title: SEGMENT 12 — The FDA Expanded Access pathway and the ethics review

lines.text: SEGMENT 12 — The FDA Expanded Access pathway and the ethics review

Let me spend a segment on the regulatory and ethical framework, because the technical achievement of designing milasen would have been moot if no regulator had been willing to permit its administration to a child.
In the United States, drugs intended for human use must, normally, be approved by the FDA on the basis of extensive clinical trial data demonstrating safety and efficacy.
The standard pathway from a new molecule to an approved drug involves Phase 1, Phase 2, and Phase 3 trials, typically involving thousands of patients and a decade or more of testing.
Milasen could not possibly go through that pathway.
There was, by definition, no patient population other than Mila.
There would be no Phase 1, Phase 2, or Phase 3 trial, because the drug was for one person.
The pathway that was used instead is called Expanded Access, sometimes called compassionate use.

Expanded Access is a regulatory mechanism by which the FDA can authorize the use of an investigational drug — a drug not yet approved for general use — in a specific patient with a serious or life-threatening condition who has no other satisfactory treatment options.
Normally, Expanded Access is used to provide a drug that is already in clinical development for some population to an individual patient who falls outside the trial criteria but might benefit.

In milasen's case, Expanded Access was used in a more radical way: to authorize the administration of a drug that had been developed specifically and exclusively for this one patient, and that was not in clinical development for anyone else.
For this to happen, the team had to submit, to the FDA, an Investigational New Drug application — an IND — covering the use of milasen in Mila.

The IND included the molecular rationale for the drug, the in-vitro validation in Mila's fibroblasts, the rat toxicology study, the proposed dosing regimen, the consent and monitoring plan, and a discussion of the risks and potential benefits.
In January of 2018, the FDA reviewed the IND and granted permission to proceed.
That permission was specific to Mila.
Milasen was not approved as a drug.
It was approved for use in this one named patient under Expanded Access.
The legal status of the molecule, as a drug, did not change.
What changed was the FDA's specific authorization that it could be administered to this one patient under defined conditions.
In parallel with the FDA submission, the case was reviewed by the institutional review board — the IRB — at Boston Children's Hospital.

The IRB is the body responsible for reviewing all research and experimental clinical activities at the institution to ensure that they meet ethical standards: that the risks have been properly characterized and minimized, that the potential benefits justify the risks, that the patient and family have given informed consent, that the science is sound enough to justify the attempt.
The IRB had to be satisfied not just that milasen was technically reasonable, but that this was an ethically appropriate use of investigational medicine.
The ethical issues were not trivial, and I want to walk through them honestly, because they will recur for every patient considered for an individualized therapy.
First, the question of informed consent.
Mila, at age six, could not give consent herself.

Her parents could give consent on her behalf, but they were also the family that had been pushing for this treatment to happen — they had founded the Mila's Miracle Foundation, raised significant funds, and been instrumental in connecting with Yu's lab.

Their consent was real and freely given, but the IRB had to be careful that the family's understandable desperation to do something for their child did not override their ability to assess the risks soberly.
The team and the family worked through extensive discussions about what was known, what was unknown, and what the realistic best- and worst-case outcomes might be.
Second, the question of benefit.
Milasen had never been given to a human.
The preclinical work suggested it would correct splicing in Mila's specific genotype and might therefore slow the progression of her disease.
But there was no guarantee that the in-vitro effect would translate to the brain.
There was no guarantee that even a perfectly corrected MFSD8 splicing pattern at this stage of her disease would slow neurodegeneration in a clinically meaningful way.
The drug might do nothing.
It might cause harm.
The IRB had to weigh those possibilities against the certain trajectory of untreated CLN7 — a relentless decline ending in death.
Third, the question of broader implications.
If the IRB approved milasen for Mila, it was implicitly creating a precedent.
Other families with rare disease children would, on hearing about this case, approach Boston Children's and similar institutions asking for similar one-off treatments.
How would the institution handle that demand?
On what basis would some patients be offered individualized therapy and others not?
Was it ethically defensible to develop a drug only for the patients whose families could fund the work and connect with the right researchers?
These were not theoretical questions.

They became, immediately after milasen was reported, very real institutional questions that Boston Children's responded to by establishing a formal Oversight Committee on Personalized Experimental Therapeutics, which now reviews all such proposals.
I want to highlight one more point about the regulatory model, because it is now becoming a template.
The FDA, in approving milasen under Expanded Access, did not commit itself to approving every individualized ASO that might be proposed in the future.

It made a case-specific judgment that this particular drug, for this particular patient, with this particular safety package and this particular molecular rationale, met the standard for compassionate use.

The pathway it used — Expanded Access, with an abbreviated preclinical program leveraging platform safety data from a precedent drug — has since been applied to additional individualized ASOs developed for other patients.

The FDA has been working with organizations like the n-Lorem Foundation to standardize, as much as possible, the framework for these submissions, so that what was unprecedented in 2018 has become, in 2024 and beyond, a defined regulatory pathway that other patients can be considered under.
The key principle that the milasen case established, regulatorily, is that the unit of analysis can, for sufficiently grave and rare conditions, legitimately shrink to a single patient.
A drug does not have to be a population-level intervention to be considered.
The FDA can, when the circumstances justify it, consider the risks and benefits of a single use of an investigational drug in a single patient, and authorize that use on a case-by-case basis.
Before milasen, this had been done for drugs that were already in development for broader populations.
After milasen, it has been done for drugs developed for one patient only.
That is a real and meaningful change in the regulatory landscape.
Alright.
The drug was designed.
The FDA had approved its use under Expanded Access.
The IRB had approved the protocol.
The family had consented.
The first dose was scheduled.
Let me tell you what happened next.
